08 Weekly Check Ins - YouTube.mp4
If quarterly is about strategic planning and monthly is about making those reviews and giving yourself the opportunity to pivot, then weekly is all about the check-in. Team check-in meetings generally take the form of a team scorecard review meeting, which is also known as a team metrics meeting. And this should be done at both the individual team level as well as... the leadership team level. So what this means is at the team level, they're going to be reviewing the team scorecard. And at the leadership level, you and your fellow leaders are going to be reviewing the company scorecard that we talked about in a previous module. So, if you're on the leadership team, you're going to probably attend two weekly review meetings.
You're going to attend it for the company as a whole, and you're going to attend it for the team that you lead. With rare exception, major changes and pivots should not be made during weekly check-ins. These kind of decisions are reserved for the monthly business review, and quarterly sprint planning meetings, which should never be more than a few weeks away, okay? So people can wait. Now, if there's a major issue that simply can't wait until the next MBR or QSP, then you can schedule an ad hoc meeting. But make sure the issue is truly important enough to merit its own meeting. By using this filter, you're going to have far fewer ad hoc meetings and a lot more time to focus on the tasks at hand.
Weekly check-ins are a time to gather information, process issues, and breakthrough bottlenecks. So they should be completed in 90 minutes or less. And as a general rule, scorecard review meetings are held early in the week, usually Monday or Tuesday. Now, some teams prefer to batch all their meetings in one day and others like to kind of spread them out. There really isn't one perfect way to do it, but the The key is to be consistent. Put a day and a time on the calendar and, barring any major holidays or world events, hold those meetings at the exact same time each and every day. Every week now we're going to cover how to facilitate a team scorecard review meeting in another lesson, but here's the basic agenda so you know how it works first.
Everyone opens with a big personal or professional win from the previous week. This sets a positive tone for the meeting and allows team members to kind of get to know one another. Second, the scorecard is reviewed and any yellow, orange, and red metrics are discussed. Third, projects and key initiatives are reviewed and discussed, especially any projects that are currently behind schedule. Fourth, everyone reports on their big three for the coming week. These are the three most important items on their to-do list, as well as the status of their big threes from the previous week. Week. Now, this is not only good for accountability, but it also gives everyone an opportunity to question one another's priorities and double check that everyone's actions and activities are aligned to the team's goals and metrics.
Fifth, the team processes any bottlenecks and opportunities that came up during the scorecard review meeting. And once everything has been processed, or. Once you've simply run out of time, the meeting ends. Again, this meeting should only last 60 to 90 minutes. If it goes longer, that's usually a sign that you're shifting into strategic planning mode, or that another ad hoc meeting should probably be scheduled at a different time. The other recurring meetings that should occur on a weekly rhythm include the company-wide all-hands meeting and that's really important, especially when your team is less than 50. Employee one-on-ones, as the name suggests, are a 30 to 60 minute meetings held once a week or every other week between a manager and a member of their team.
These meetings are time-consuming, but they're worth it because they provide your team members with a venue to discuss their particular challenges as well as opportunities for growth and improvement. One-on-ones and all-hands meetings will be covered in a supplemental video. training, but as a general rule, if you have a weekly team metrics meeting, a weekly all-hands meeting, and your managers are all holding weekly or every other week one-on-ones with their direct reports, then congratulations. Your company-wide meeting rhythm. Quarterly. Monthly and now weekly is officially complete. So right now, while it's fresh in your mind, take a minute and schedule out your quarterly sprint plans, monthly business review. And even your team and company-wide all-hands meetings for the next four quarters. If you do, you'll see that there is still a massive amount of calendar space left to do 'quote-unquote' real work. But remember, strategic planning. Scorecard reviews, all-hands meetings, and one-on-ones, these all count as real work. In fact, as your company scales, it just might be the most important work that you do.
